blocks.content: The early part was careful. Maya asked about his work. He answered without deflecting. She asked how he'd met Cassandra — he told the truth: she'd walked up to him at a fountain, interrupted something he'd been doing privately, and he'd been glad she had.
Maya looked at the table when he said that. Something moved through her face and went away.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: She does that. She finds people in the middle of things. She always has. Even as a kid — she'd go straight for the one at a birthday party sitting near the wall, the adult at a family dinner who was answering questions too quickly. She has a sense for it.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: What does she find in them?

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Which is why I imagine she found quite a lot in you.
He didn't deny it. Just acknowledged it.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Can I ask you something directly?

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: How well do you know her? Not the day-to-day of her — I mean the parts that are harder to show. How much of that have you seen?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I know she lives like the moment she's in is the only one that matters. I know that when she says something it lands differently than when most people say things, because she means it completely. I know there's something she hasn't told me yet. I don't know what it is. But I can feel the shape of something around it.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: It makes me want to stay until she's ready to say it.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Because she's the first person in a long time who made me want to be present instead of beside something. And that doesn't come with conditions.
Maya looked at him for a long moment. Then she looked at her coffee. Then back at him, and this time with something different in her expression — not less guarded exactly, but more real.
